skill.name: azure-storage-blob-ts
skill.description: TypeScript and JavaScript guidance for Azure Blob Storage blob, container, SAS token, streaming, upload, and download workflows.
skill.summary: Build Azure Blob Storage workflows with the JavaScript and TypeScript SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The scanner reported many command execution hits, but review shows they are Markdown fences and inline TypeScript examples, not executable skill logic. The remaining risk is legitimate but sensitive: examples access Azure credentials, construct Azure Blob endpoints, transfer local files, and generate broad SAS tokens.

medium_findings.title: Credential-backed Azure Blob operations
medium_findings.description: The examples read Azure account names, connection strings, account keys, and SAS tokens from environment variables. This is legitimate SDK guidance, but generated code could access live storage accounts if users provide credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows direct process.env access for Azure credentials and endpoint construction. The use is expected for this SDK skill, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local file upload and download examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that upload local files and streams to Azure Blob Storage and download blobs to local paths. Users should review paths and data sensitivity before running generated code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit uploadFile, fs.createReadStream, and downloadToFile examples. These are normal storage operations but can move local data across a network.

medium_findings.title: Broad SAS permission examples
medium_findings.description: The SAS examples include container permissions for read, add, create, write, delete, and list, plus an account SAS with many permissions. These examples can create overbroad access if copied without restriction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permissions strings are visible in the documented SAS examples. The examples are not malicious, but they can encourage overly broad delegated access.

low_findings.title: Static command execution findings are Markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The many backtick findings correspond to Markdown code fences and inline API names. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or shell command execution in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown fences or TypeScript snippets, not executable Ruby or shell syntax. The file is a single Markdown skill document with no runnable script files.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are scanner false positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography lines do not show cryptographic algorithm use. They occur in front matter or blob copy and download examples, so no weak hash or cipher evidence was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe Azure Blob Storage and SDK operations. They do not contain MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic calls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment credential examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples read storage credentials from process.env for connection strings, account keys, and SAS tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process.env credential references are explicit. This is legitimate cloud SDK usage but should be treated as sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network endpoint construction
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples construct Azure Blob Storage URLs using the account name and optional SAS token.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs target the standard Azure Blob Storage service. This is expected behavior, with risk limited to user-authorized network access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem transfer examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show uploading local files and streams and downloading blobs into local files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The APIs directly read or write filesystem paths. The behavior is normal for blob storage tooling but can expose local data if misused.

content.value_statement: Teams need reliable code guidance for Azure Blob Storage operations. This skill provides TypeScript and JavaScript patterns for authentication, containers, blobs, SAS tokens, and streams.
actual_capabilities: Explains BlobServiceClient, ContainerClient, BlobClient, and specialized blob clients.
Provides authentication patterns for DefaultAzureCredential, connection strings, account keys, and SAS tokens.
Shows upload and download flows for strings, buffers, browser files, streams, and local files.
Documents container creation, listing, deletion, and idempotent creation methods.
Covers SAS token generation for blobs, containers, and storage accounts.
Summarizes platform differences between Node.js and browser SDK usage.
limitations: It is a reference skill, not a runnable Azure client or deployment tool.
It does not validate credentials, storage account permissions, or network connectivity.
Examples use placeholder paths and container names that must be adapted.
Security settings still require review against your Azure policies.

use_cases.title: Implement blob upload flows
use_cases.description: Create TypeScript upload logic for browser files, buffers, streams, or local Node.js files.

use_cases.title: Review storage access patterns
use_cases.description: Compare credential options and select safer authentication for backend or client workflows.

use_cases.title: Prototype data movement scripts
use_cases.description: Draft focused scripts for listing containers, copying blobs, downloading files, or setting metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic upload example
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single blob upload.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-storage-blob-ts skill to show a simple TypeScript upload flow for one text blob. Prefer DefaultAzureCredential and explain required environment values.

prompt_templates.title: Build a container workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-storage-blob-ts skill to design a TypeScript workflow that creates a container if needed, lists blobs with a prefix, and deletes one blob safely.

prompt_templates.title: Generate short-lived SAS access
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-storage-blob-ts skill to draft a least-privilege SAS token plan for browser uploads. Include permissions, expiration, and server-side generation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design a streaming transfer service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-storage-blob-ts skill to plan a Node.js streaming upload and download service for large files. Include concurrency, progress reporting, error handling, and security checks.

output_examples.input: Request: Explain how to upload a local file to Azure Blob Storage in Node.js.
output_examples.output: The response outlines credential setup, client creation, container selection, local path review, upload execution, and error handling considerations.

output_examples.input: Request: Compare connection strings, shared keys, SAS tokens, and DefaultAzureCredential.
output_examples.output: The response compares each authentication method, recommends identity-based access where possible, and warns against exposing long-lived credentials.

output_examples.input: Request: Plan a short-lived read-only SAS URL for one blob.
output_examples.output: The response describes blob-scoped permissions, a short expiration window, server-side generation, and safe delivery to the requesting client.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential for server workloads and managed Azure environments.
Use least-privilege SAS permissions with short expiration times.
Review local file paths and blob names before running generated transfer code.
anti_patterns: Do not place storage account keys or connection strings in browser code.
Do not generate account-level SAS tokens when a blob-level token is enough.
Do not copy examples with placeholder paths into production without validation.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown reference that guides code generation for the Azure Storage Blob SDK.

faq.question: Which package does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers @azure/storage-blob and related identity usage through @azure/identity.

faq.question: Can it help with browser uploads?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes browser upload patterns and explains why SAS access should be generated server-side.

faq.question: Does it support large file transfers?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents stream uploads, file downloads, buffers, and progress callbacks for Node.js workflows.

faq.question: Does it create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. Generated code can create containers or blobs only after a user runs it with valid credentials.

faq.question: What should users review before running generated code?
faq.answer: Users should review credentials, SAS permissions, local file paths, blob names, and target storage accounts.
